author: @AdmiralStoicRum
text: So before I started to watch this particular episode my first thought was you could pack Crisco in the interior can to maybe about 80% full maybe a little bit less, and then kind of you know rub a bit on your your wick Then when you light the Crisco the heat will of course transfer through the carbon wick start heating the solid Crisco and then you know then it'll start putting a liquid out for for there to be drawn up. This is before I've watched the video so I would like to see if that holds true.

author: @Mixwell1983
text:
Ive found that using 99% rubbing alcohol eventually i get soot all over the mantle thus coating it and mever get the radiant heat.  I have to rinse the soot off for it to work again. 

I love using methanol but it's more expensive.

author: @Mixwell1983
text:
The most brilliant thing is the carbon felt.  The fact that unlike cotton it wont degrade is huge.  I have a few old lamp oil lanters and i wonder of i made a wick the same dimensions and used it if it would work 

Ive been using brake cleaner as my starter because it contains methanol (think i mentioned this) but I did make a heater from 1746 that didn't start and ive notice pure rubbing alcohol is slow to catch.  My 8 year old was excited to see the heater and mentioned how long it took.  Short of using lighter fluid i remembered i had brake part cleaner from replacing my rotors and it worked perfectly so i use a few sprays on the outter wick to get it goin.

If lamp oil isn't too sooty I can use the brake cleaner as a starter to get it going.

Again rubbing alcohol is nice but burns up wayy tooo fast for me.

author: @Mixwell1983
text:
Essentially what this heater ends up acting as is a mini version of those tall standing patio heaters with the mesh screen.  I love the design and I do have a propane circular heating gas firepit.  Im wondering if i pieced together enough wire mesh and a large topper for it of it would act as a radiant heater.  I wouldn't have a surround on it but i wonder of the heat comes from the mantle being directly underneath the carbon felt.

This could potentially upscale the heater idea with something i already have. I wish to find the mesh in a roll but can only find it on amazon in smaller sheets roughly the size of a piece of printer paper.

author: @Mixwell1983
text:
My heater works well with 91% rubbing alcohol but it burns about a pint an hour.  I am thinking about trying lamp oil and using brake cleaner which contains methanol as the starter but don't want the flame to be really sooty.  I need something that is more efficient than what i have.

I did drill a coin sized hole in the top to add fuel like in video 1700 but closed it off with a bolt and washer.  My suspicion is that even though the hole is covered there could be some air gaps which is making it burn from the middle.  I dont see an active flame but sometimes the flame around the can does seem to have some in the middle.

Not sure if that's what's causing it but it was tough enough to get the inner can and carbon felt to slide into the outer can that i don't want to take it apart each time i fill the canister..

author: @MajorMalfunction
text:
If you burn oil indoors everything will get covered in soot. I learnt the hard way. I had a little scented oil lamp I used while playing my computer games. One night my computer stopped working. I turned on the light, and the whole room was coated in soot. Even the spider webs. I opened the computer, and inside was covered in soot, too.
Soot being mostly pure carbon is an excellent conductor, which isn't very good for electronics. Or your lungs.

author: @jamesross1003
text: Just a thought, for the heavier fuels use a wick and gravity.  For example raise the heavier fuels up and externally wick them into your burner in close enough proximity to preheat them.  This would require a much more careful approach as it would have more potential for accidents to happen, but may be a solution to the heavier fuel difficulties.  It is what I had to do with my wax pulse jet engine.  It does work though it is a much more complex setup.  Loving these videos Rob!  Keep them coming!

replies.author: @lagunafishing
replies.text: Agree, pre-heating the heavier oils will make it burn leaner. Good idea on the gravity feed system too... maybe fed by a copper coil wrapped around the stove?

replies.author: @jamesross1003
replies.text: @lagunafishing Pretty much what I did with the jet.  Sat a container about 2 inches over the middle of the exhaust and wrapped a copper tube around the exhaust and fed it into the combustion chamber into the intake end of it.  When heated up to full heat, it basically gasified the wax as it left the tube into the chamber.  To get it started I burned methanol.  Then changed it over to a mix of methanol and wax to extend fuel burn time.  Only gave about 4 lbs of thrust but it worked as a proof of concept.

author: @alexwild4350
text:
I think Robert, the UK Government is ahead of you here. A previous commentator started you off on this journey by raising the issue, if I remember right, about there being little point to a wonderful heater if the parts to make it are difficult to obtain.
I hold the view that is only one part of the equation.
If the fuels are expensive to obtain there is little point to having this style of heater.
Thus the UK Government already puts the price point of Alcohols high, and indeed anything Spirit based, White Spirit, Petroleum Spirit etc, are similarly high.  The Government have gone to excessive lengths to make, can't remember if its Turpentine, taste awful and be Purple by colour to stop people drinking it for its Alcohol content. The last time I casually looked for Turpentine [probably the wrong name] in B&Q, it was almost £5 for 500ml. Hardly a viable fuel source for long term heating. Behind this Spirits and Alcohols are by their nature more dangerous than Heavy Oils for being ignited by unwanted sources. Even Parafin in the UK is price loaded, before the Diesel price hikes of recent, to make it more expensive than Diesel oil because it could be used in a road vehicle, despite it being used for heating in the main. Thanks Government.

From a practical perspective, there are many options as you demonstrate, but from a financial and construction point of view, there are very few viable answers. Most of those answers require a flu or Chimney to be reliably safe, leaving only Parafin that does not, but only if you can reliably achieve a blue flame. The UK Government has successfully outlawed one's ability to be warm on the cheap.

Thus from a 'fuel and financial first' perspective, it is probably Wood from the woodlands, Charcoal, Coal, heavy oils cheaply and easily obtainable from the Supermarket, before we get into Spirits and Alcohols.
Much of this has already been researched over the last 200 hundred years by millions of people, which goes to show the real progress you've made is through materials engineering, the Everlasting Wick. But beyond that, its just re-inventing the wheel, or stove.

The UK public is of course trying to do things as the cart is put before the horse. A Rocket Stove, wood buring, with Mass Storage is a great answer, but could most people effectively fit one into their existing built homes ? Moreover, people who burn wood stoves buy their wood by the ton. Where do you store it if your in a flat ? This is what I mean by meeting the financial and practical objectives from the start, and it really begins with designing your home for alternative fuels from the start, rather than retrospectively.
In Siberia they use the Rocket Stove with Mass Storage. Need we say more. There is an ever lasting supply of wood on their door step.

author: @matthewellisor5835
text:
For the heavier oils a short and broad can makes for a nice pre-heater, using a few wraps for the mantle and extending it into the oil gives more hearing of the fuel and more surface area for complete combustion. adding airflow in the center of the stove would likely help but that seems to me to be getting away from the spirit of the simple device.

One other note: Paraffin is also the name for a wax (as for candle making or some canning uses) in many areas, paraffin oil is sold as kerosene or lamp oil in some locations.

replies.author: @technicalfool
replies.text: RC engines run on a mix of methanol and castor or some other oil, often with a percentage of nitromethane to give car engines a bit of extra kick. Basically yes, but be aware that it burns with an almost invisible flame, so be careful!

author: @malHHkenny
text: Heavy vegetable fuels burn much longer by weigh and are available at any grocery store. So, getting a good big flame from them would be terrific. Cheap, available, compact, don't require constant attention, visible flame, some toxicity, wimpy flame. <sigh> A way to get adequate air into the propagation mixture?

replies.author: @malHHkenny
replies.text: It would be good to have an outside-the-tent heater (a fresh-air tube suspended over flame, with the open end to the air and the other end of the tube venting heated air into the tent) if that wasn't based on a wood fire. Putting a small metal tube through the wall of a tent is easy. Having a distant end of that tube heating over a fire and drawing cold fresh air is easy enough. A wood fire is sub-optimal, to say the least.

replies.author: @malHHkenny
replies.text: (Some would prefer recirculating hot water rather than a tube of fresh hot air to heat a small space. Same serious downside on having to use a wood fire. Lotsa plumbing.)

replies.author: @malHHkenny
replies.text: Even a tiny stove of any kind inside a tent is too dangerous.

replies.author: @pa_maj.MARTINI-van-MAN
replies.text: @malHHkenny I've seen this idea on another channel a couple of days ago, yes he used a fire, using vegetable oil or diesel Kenny would be Good as it could burn all night with the right setup. Just heated fresh air entering the tent, what's not to like about that. With more pipework and a small low energy fan, heated air from the tent could be recirculated around the system for better efficiency, Great for a base tent and similar to how my eberspacher diesel night heater works in my campervan.

replies.author: @malHHkenny
replies.text: @pa_maj.MARTINI-van-MAN Tilting the tube to the perfect angle does as well or even better than a fan. It would be good to experiment with various tubing diameters. (BTW: Only the part of the tube in the flame has to be fireproof. 2 feet from there, it's just nicely hot, tending toward warm. Weight savings.) But yeah, what's not to like about totally fresh air heat and having the fire outside?

author: @McRootbeer
text: 3 ideas: 1.  Increase the mantle thickness using 2 or 3 wraps of soffit mesh to increase the surface area of hot metal for the gasified oil to burn against.  2.  Wrap the outer can in insulation to improve preheating.  3.  Stick a pipe through the center of the burner to increase airflow and improve preheating.

Kindest Regards and Best Wishes, 
Andrea and Critters. ...XxX...

author: @ile84

author: @NothingByHalves
text:
Getting confused between the different oils and what is safe to burn inside the house.
I've ordered carbon felt to make a forever wick (found carbon content 98% + - none of the others defined the content and the description for use sounded fit for purpose)
I was planning to use the forever wick with vegetable oil in a double pot heater but not sure about a) CO emissions and b) black soot.  
I can test in the kitchen (with a CO alarm) but when you first demonstrated the forever wick the flame was yellow and the smoke black... 
Not planning to die from cold this winter, but don't plan to take myself off the planet trying to stay warm 😁
Brilliant channel Robert.  Love your enthusiasm!  That's what we need!

author: @Jollyprez
text: Heavier oils need WICKS. I have a kerosene stove from the 1940s which uses edge-burning asbestos wicks - burns blue with virtually no smoke after its going.

replies.author: @TheWingnut58
replies.text: He's using carbon felt as the WICK

replies.author: @Jollyprez
replies.text: @TheWingnut58 Wrong kind of wick, he needs edge-burner not a sponge. Oil stoves and furnaces were doing this 100 years ago.

replies.author: @TheWingnut58
replies.text:
First,  "wick" is an action,  not a specific object 
Second, this entire line of experiments was to test a "forever wick".....most of us already know how  traditional wicks function

replies.author: @Jollyprez
replies.text: @TheWingnut58 First, "wick" is ALSO a noun! Jeez. I have a LOT of experience with kerosene and oil burners - I was just trying to shed a little light. I, too, am designing small heaters, but my fuel of choice is #1 or #2 oils.

replies.author: @TheWingnut58
replies.text:
I know that "wick" can be used as a noun too.....but you're still missing the point.
The purpose of the experiment was to TEST CARBON FELT as a wick material.....this fact makes your original comment a MOOT POINT.....

author: @ManceUKBOA
text: petrol ⛽?

replies.author: @technicalfool
replies.text:
That would be one to try outdoors. Petrol (gasoline for the few Americans who may not know) is incredibly lightweight and has a tendency to evaporate and explode, especially if it were to get really hot in a heater like that. I've had that happen in my younger and more stupid days enough times to have a healthy respect for the stuff. It's why every bag of barbeque coals tells you to specifically NOT use petrol to ignite it. You've every risk of a vapour explosion blasting flaming charcoal over a wide area!

I guess you could find some kind of design that would work with lighter fractions like petrol and naptha. I'm just not sure a bean tin and some carbon foam would be a wise choice.

replies.author: @Milkybar3320011
replies.text: @technicalfool spot on, a fine line between a burn and a bang 😆

author: @NM-wd7kx
text:
I've been experimenting with a meths penny stove, have you tried a conical or enclosed mantle?

I've had a bit of luck with some wire wool atop the burner, but I doubt that'll last more than a single use so I'll be trying wire scourers next, probably in a chopped up tea strainer.

I've also been considering a few copper air pipes, just to add airflow to the base of the system, though if the mantle works out that shouldn't be necessary

replies.author: @l0I0I0I0
replies.text: Controlling the air has a HUGE effect on how fast the fuel flows.  A slow burning fuels heat can actually be regulated by turn air flow off and on.

replies.author: @NM-wd7kx
replies.text: @l0I0I0I0 my thinking was both to preheat the air & to deliver it to the base of the fire, like you would with a twig burner or a rocket stove, but for a fluid fuel.

replies.author: @l0I0I0I0
replies.text: @NM-wd7kx Interesting.  Not sure how it will work, but would be interesting.  I know with jet engines cold air is denser providing more Oxygen, though at the same time warmer air helps with the heat.  I suspect the dense cold air with slightly higher O2 due to density would trump benefits of higher temp.  But idk for certain.

replies.author: @pa_maj.MARTINI-van-MAN
replies.text:
Stainless Steel scourers work well (glowing red and/or bright yellow) if loosely pulled apart, less is more if you know what I mean. The Stainless Steel is tough and lasts (I've used it with an ethanol burner before".
I was actually going to try a tea strainer placed over a gas ring (gas cooker flame) upside down (dome pointing up) then place a reflector behind it, I recon that would work Good, very similar to some manufactured heaters NM.

replies.author: @NM-wd7kx
replies.text: @pa_maj.MARTINI-van-MAN have you run into those gas hob 'energy savers' that sit atop your hob & heat up a lattice of wires that radiate then radiate the heat from there?
